content.text:
# The 2D Fourier transform The **2D Fourier transform** takes an image $g(x,y)$ and rewrites it as a sum of **complex 2D plane waves**. Each plane wave has the form $e^{2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}$ — a sinusoid that ripples across the image plane in some direction at some spatial frequency. The transform tells you, for every possible $(f_x, f_y)$, *how much* of that wave is present and *where* it sits. So instead of a grid of pixel intensities, you get a grid of **complex numbers indexed by frequency**: one number per $(f_x, f_y)$. That number's **magnitude** is how strongly that wave shows up in the image, and its **phase** is where the wave is positioned (its offset). Both pieces are needed to reconstruct the image — but when we *visualize* a Fourier spectrum we usually only show $|\hat{g}|$, since phase doesn't render as anything human-readable. ## The transform pair Forward (image → spectrum): $$\hat{g}(f_x, f_y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} g(x,y)\, e^{-2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, dx\, dy$$ Inverse (spectrum → image): $$g(x,y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \hat{g}(f_x, f_y)\, e^{2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, df_x\, df_y$$ The minus sign in the forward transform and the plus in the inverse is just a sign convention — the two transforms are nearly identical, which is one reason Fourier theory is so symmetric. ## What one Fourier-space pixel actually represents This is the bit that confuses most people on the first pass, so it's worth pinning down: - **One pixel in Fourier space depends on every pixel in the original image.** That's because the integral runs over all $(x,y)$. - **Conversely, one pixel in real space contributes to every pixel in Fourier space.** Each spatial sample is built from a contribution at every frequency. So Fourier space is **not** a spatial map. A bright spot at $(f_x, f_y) = (0.1, 0)$ doesn't mean "something interesting happens at that location" — it means *the image as a whole contains a strong horizontal stripe pattern at frequency 0.1 cycles per pixel*. ## The frequency grid and `fftshift` The discrete FFT returns frequencies in a peculiar order. For $N = 7$ samples, the raw FFT bins come out as $$[0,\; \tfrac{1}{7},\; \tfrac{2}{7},\; \tfrac{3}{7},\; -\tfrac{3}{7},\; -\tfrac{2}{7},\; -\tfrac{1}{7}]$$ — DC first, then positive frequencies climbing, then negative frequencies wrapping back. After applying `fftshift` they get reordered into the natural, monotonic layout $$[-\tfrac{3}{7},\; -\tfrac{2}{7},\; -\tfrac{1}{7},\; 0,\; \tfrac{1}{7},\; \tfrac{2}{7},\; \tfrac{3}{7}].$$ In 2D this puts the **DC term in the centre of the spectrum image**, which is the layout every spectrum visualization assumes. If you ever look at a Fourier image and the bright spot is in the corner instead of the middle, you forgot the shift. ## Radial structure: why spectra look like ringed blobs Frequencies with the same magnitude $$|f| = \sqrt{f_x^2 + f_y^2}$$ form **concentric rings** in Fourier space. This gives a clean physical interpretation: - **Centre = low frequencies.** Slow variations: overall brightness, large-scale shapes, gradients. - **Outer rings = high frequencies.** Edges, fine detail, texture, noise. Natural images have a famous statistical regularity: the **average magnitude on each ring decays roughly as $1/\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$**. The DC term (the average pixel value) is enormous, and energy falls off quickly as you move outward. That's why if you `imshow` a raw spectrum it looks like a single white dot in a black void — the centre saturates the colormap. The fix is to plot $\log(A+1)$ instead, which compresses the dynamic range and reveals the structure. ## Pipeline view ```mermaid flowchart LR img(["image g(x,y)"]) -->|"FFT2"| raw["raw spectrum
(DC in corner)"] raw -->|"fftshift"| spec(["ĝ(f_x, f_y)
DC in centre"]) spec -->|"|·|, log"| vis["log-magnitude image
concentric rings visible"] spec -->|"phase"| ph["phase image
(rarely visualized)"] classDef out fill:#e3f2fd class spec out ``` ## Why this matters The 2D FT is a foundation, not an end in itself. Once you have it, several spatial-domain operations become **multiplication of complex numbers in Fourier space**, which is faster, cleaner, and exposes structure that's invisible in pixel space. The downstream tools the lecture builds on this base — [[low-pass-filter]], [[high-pass-filter]], [[band-pass-filter]], the [[convolution-theorem]], the [[fourier-shift-theorem]], spectrum-based [[fourier-resizing]], [[deconvolution]], [[wiener-deconvolution]], and the [[nyquist-theorem]] — all assume you've internalized the 2D FT first. --- *The lecture frames the 2D FT as a direct generalisation of the 1D case: the integral $\hat{g}(f_x, f_y) = \int g(x,y)\, e^{-2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, dx\, dy$ decomposes the image into plane waves, with each Fourier-space pixel being one complex number depending on **all** pixels of the input image, and each input pixel contributing to **all** Fourier pixels. The slides emphasise the `fftshift` convention (centring DC), the radial reading of the spectrum (low frequency at centre, high frequency at edge), and the $1/\sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$ falloff that motivates log-magnitude display. Source: `10_fourier_transform2.pdf`, slides 1–7; see [[fourier-transform-2d]] and `10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/lecture-10-fourier-transform-2.md`.* **Remember this:** The 2D FT writes $g(x,y)$ as a sum of complex plane waves $e^{2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}$; each Fourier-space pixel is one complex number (magnitude + phase) encoding how strongly one frequency $(f_x, f_y)$ is present across the *entire* image. **Beyond the lecture:** I emphasised the "phase encodes position, magnitude encodes strength" framing more explicitly than the slides do — a useful intuition is that **phase carries most of the perceptual content of an image** (if you swap two images' magnitudes but keep their phases, you'll still recognise each image from its phase). The slides mention the $1/|f|$ falloff but don't connect it to the broader fact that this is a **statistical property of natural images** (the so-called $1/f$ spectrum), studied heavily in computational neuroscience and image-prior modelling. Neither point is required for the exam, but they're the "why" behind the conventions.
I dont get it. explain it to me like im a baby

OK, baby mode. Forget the integrals. ## The core idea: every image is a stack of stripes Imagine you have a recipe. A smoothie can be broken down into "30% banana, 50% strawberry, 20% milk." If I hand you a smoothie, you could (in principle) reverse-engineer the recipe. The **Fourier transform does the same thing for images**, except the "ingredients" are not fruits — they're **striped wave patterns**. Every image you can imagine — a face, a cat, a chessboard, a noisy mess — can be built by stacking up enough striped patterns and adding them together. The Fourier transform's job is to **figure out the recipe**: how much of each stripe pattern is in your image. ## What does a "stripe pattern" look like? A stripe pattern has two things going on: 1. **A direction** — vertical stripes, horizontal stripes, diagonal stripes, anything in between. 2. **A spacing** — fat stripes (low frequency) or thin stripes (high frequency). That's it. Every Fourier "ingredient" is just a pattern of light-to-dark-to-light bands going in some direction with some thickness. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["fat horizontal stripes
(low freq, vertical direction)"] --> M["+"] B["thin vertical stripes
(high freq, horizontal direction)"] --> M C["medium diagonal stripes"] --> M D["...thousands more..."] --> M M --> IMG(["your image"]) ``` If you mix the right amounts of the right stripes, you get back the original image. **Exactly.** No approximation, no information lost. ## Why two numbers $(f_x, f_y)$? Because a stripe pattern has a direction, you need **two numbers** to describe it: - $f_x$ = how fast the pattern wiggles as you move **left-right** across the image. - $f_y$ = how fast it wiggles as you move **up-down**. If $f_x$ is big and $f_y = 0$ → thin **vertical stripes** (the pattern wiggles fast left-right, doesn't change as you go up-down). If $f_y$ is big and $f_x = 0$ → thin **horizontal stripes**. If both are equal → **diagonal** stripes. So the pair $(f_x, f_y)$ picks out one specific stripe pattern. **The Fourier transform tells you "how much of this stripe pattern is in my image"** for every possible $(f_x, f_y)$. ## The "one Fourier pixel = whole image" thing This is the part you said you don't get, so let's do it slowly. When you ask the question *"how much of this thin-vertical-stripe pattern is in my picture of a cat?"* — you can't answer that by looking at one corner of the cat. You have to scan the **entire image**, see how well that stripe pattern lines up with what's there, and add it all up. **One number out — but the input was the whole image.** That's all the lecture means by "one pixel in Fourier space depends on all pixels in real space." It's not weird. It's just: you can't measure a global pattern by peeking at one spot. ## What does the spectrum picture actually show? When you see those black images with a bright cross or a glowing centre — that's a **map of the recipe**. Each spot in that map is "how much of one stripe pattern is in the image." - **Centre of the map** = fat, slow stripes (the big-picture stuff: overall brightness, large shapes). - **Edges of the map** = thin, fast stripes (fine details, edges, noise). - **Bright spot** = "lots of this stripe pattern is in the image." - **Dark spot** = "barely any of this stripe pattern." Most natural images have a really bright **centre** (lots of slow variation — the average colour, the rough shapes) and dim outer rings (less fine detail than coarse structure). That's the famous "$1/f$ falloff" — and it's why the centre saturates and you have to plot $\log$ of the magnitude to see anything else. ## Why anyone bothers Once you have your image written as a recipe of stripes, a bunch of operations get **way easier**: - Want to **blur** the image? Throw away the thin-stripe ingredients (high frequencies) and rebuild. Done. - Want to **sharpen** it? Boost the thin-stripe ingredients. - Want to **shift** it sideways? You don't even have to move pixels — just nudge the recipe. You'll see all of this in the rest of lecture 10. But the core unlock is: **stop thinking of an image as a grid of pixels, start thinking of it as a recipe of stripes.** --- *The lecture's actual framing: $\hat{g}(f_x, f_y) = \int g(x,y)\, e^{-2\pi i (x f_x + y f_y)}\, dx\, dy$ — each $(f_x, f_y)$ gives one complex number; one Fourier pixel depends on all real-space pixels and vice versa; `fftshift` puts DC at centre; magnitudes on rings of equal $|f| = \sqrt{f_x^2+f_y^2}$ decay roughly as $1/|f|$ in natural images. Source: `10_fourier_transform2.pdf`, slides 1–7.* **Remember this:** Every image = a recipe of striped wave patterns; the 2D Fourier transform tells you how much of each stripe pattern (one per direction-and-thickness combo) is in your image. **Beyond the lecture:** The "stripes" framing is mine — the slides go straight to plane-wave equations. The mental model is the same thing, just dressed down. Also, "complex number" really just means "magnitude + phase" = "how much of the stripe is there + where the stripe's peaks land" — phase being the "where" is the part the equations hide behind $e^{i\theta}$.

For every lecture note in 10-Courses/Computer-Vision/lectures/, add a clickable link to the source slide PDF. Process:

For each lecture note, read the frontmatter source: field to get the PDF filename
Insert this line immediately after the H1 title (the # Lecture NN — ... line):
markdown📑 [Open slides ↗](../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/{filename}.pdf)
Replace {filename} with the actual PDF filename from frontmatter (preserving any (1) suffixes etc.).
If the line already exists in the note, skip — don't duplicate.
Leave one blank line between the title, the link, and the next section.

Show me the diff for one lecture first. After "go", do all 11.
11 lecture notes confirmed. Let me show a sample diff using lecture 11.

Sample diff for `lecture-11-feature-detectors.md` (frontmatter `source: 11_feature_detectors.pdf`):

```diff
 # Lecture 11 — Feature detectors

+📑 [Open slides ↗](../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/11_feature_detectors.pdf)
+
 ## Overview
```

A note on the template — the source-field values already include `.pdf` (e.g. `11_feature_detectors.pdf`), so I'll substitute the filename as-is rather than appending another `.pdf` (otherwise you'd get `11_feature_detectors.pdf.pdf`). Path resolves correctly: `lectures/` → `../../30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/`.

Say **go** and I'll apply to all 11.

Option B — Frontmatter property (renders as a clickable link in Obsidian's properties panel):
markdown---
tags: [lecture]
course: Computer-Vision
source: 12_feature_detectors2.pdf
lecture: 12
slides: "[[30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf|Open slides ↗]]"
---
Got it — adding a `slides` frontmatter property instead. Let me show the diff for `lecture-12-feature-detectors-2.md` (the one in your example).

Proposed diff:

```diff
 ---
 tags: [lecture]
 course: Computer-Vision
 source: 12_feature_detectors2.pdf
 lecture: 12
+slides: "[[30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/12_feature_detectors2.pdf|Open slides ↗]]"
 created: 2026-04-26
 ---
```

A couple of clarifications before I roll this out to all 11:

1. **Replace or coexist?** Are you scrapping Option A (the inline H1 link) entirely, or do you want both the frontmatter property *and* the inline link? My read is "replace" — confirm.
2. **Vault-root wikilink path** — I'll use `[[30-Sources/Computer-Vision/pdf/...]]` exactly as in your example (Obsidian resolves it from the vault root regardless of the note's location, so this works from `10-Courses/...`).
3. **Property placement** — I'll put `slides:` right after `lecture:` and before `created:` to match your example exactly.

Say **go** (and clarify replace-vs-coexist if applicable) and I'll process all 11.
Reading remaining lecture frontmatters in parallel.

bake it into the ingest workflow in claude.md

content.newText:
1. Read the PDF in `30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{NN}_{topic}.pdf`
2. Create ONE lecture note in `10-Courses/{course}/lectures/lecture-{NN}-{topic}.md`
   - Frontmatter: `tags: [lecture]`, `course: {course}`, `source: {pdf-filename}`, `lecture: NN`, `slides: "[[30-Sources/{course}/pdf/{pdf-filename}|Open slides ↗]]"`, `created: YYYY-MM-DD`
   - The `slides` value is a vault-root wikilink so Obsidian renders it as a clickable "Open slides ↗" link in the properties panel. Use the exact source filename including any `(1)` suffix and the `.pdf` extension.
   - Sections: Overview, Key concepts (linked), Equations, Diagrams, Open questions
3. Extract atomic concepts to `20-Concepts/{kebab-case-name}.md`
   - One concept per file
